intro:
A case converter instantly transforms your text between different letter case formats: uppercase, lowercase, title case, sentence case, camelCase, snake_case, and more. Instead of manually retyping text or using find-and-replace tricks, simply paste your text and select the desired case format.

Developers use case converters to transform variable names between naming conventions. Writers use them to fix incorrectly cased headings or titles. Students use them to fix accidentally caps-locked text. Content managers use them to standardize titles across a website or document library.

Our free online case converter handles all common text transformations instantly with no downloads required. It is especially useful when working with data that comes from inconsistent sources — a database dump in all-caps, copy-pasted text from a PDF, or content that needs standardized formatting before publishing.
howItWorks: Paste or type your text into the input field. Select the desired case format from the options: UPPERCASE, lowercase, Title Case, Sentence case, camelCase, PascalCase, snake_case, or kebab-case. The converted text appears instantly in the output field, ready to copy.
interpretationGuide: Each case format has its appropriate use context. Use UPPERCASE for headers and emphasis, lowercase for casual text, Title Case for book and article titles, Sentence case for normal prose, camelCase and PascalCase for programming, and snake_case or kebab-case for URLs and file names.

faqs.question: What is title case?
faqs.answer: Title case capitalizes the first letter of most words: The Quick Brown Fox Jumps Over the Lazy Dog. Small words like 'the', 'a', 'in', and 'of' are typically lowercase unless they start the title.

faqs.question: What is sentence case?
faqs.answer: Sentence case capitalizes only the first word of a sentence and proper nouns, like a normal sentence: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. It is the standard format for body text.

faqs.question: What is camelCase?
faqs.answer: camelCase joins words without spaces, capitalizing each word except the first: myVariableName. It is commonly used in JavaScript, Java, and other programming languages for variable and function names.

faqs.question: What is PascalCase?
faqs.answer: PascalCase capitalizes the first letter of every word with no spaces: MyVariableName. It is commonly used for class names in most programming languages and for component names in React.

faqs.question: What is snake_case?
faqs.answer: snake_case uses underscores between words with all letters lowercase: my_variable_name. It is the standard naming convention in Python, Ruby, and SQL, and is commonly used for database column names.

faqs.question: What is kebab-case?
faqs.answer: kebab-case uses hyphens between words with all letters lowercase: my-variable-name. It is the standard format for CSS class names, HTML attributes, URL slugs, and file names in web development.

faqs.question: How do I fix accidentally caps-locked text?
faqs.answer: Paste the ALL-CAPS text into the converter and select 'Sentence case' or 'lowercase' depending on your need. This is much faster than retyping the text and works perfectly for long passages.

faqs.question: Can I convert multiple paragraphs at once?
faqs.answer: Yes, the case converter handles any amount of text including multiple paragraphs, lists, or large documents. Paste the full text and convert it all at once.

faqs.question: Does case conversion work with numbers and symbols?
faqs.answer: Numbers and symbols are unaffected by case conversion since they have no uppercase or lowercase form. Only alphabetic characters are changed during conversion.

faqs.question: What is the difference between uppercase and capital case?
faqs.answer: They are the same thing. UPPERCASE (also called all caps or capital case) converts all letters to their capital form. It is used for acronyms, headings, emphasis, and legal document headers.
